title: Effects of Piceatannol on Melanoma. Piceatannol, a resveratrol analog found in grapes, has shown potential anti-melanoma activity. It inhibits melanogenesis in melanoma cells by suppressing tyrosinase activity and oxidative stress. Further, piceatannol was shown to induce a G2/M phase arrest in melanoma cells. A recent study has shown that piceatannol can reduce the proliferation, migration, and invasion of melanoma cells and inhibit tumor growth in a xenograft model by inhibiting the phosphorylation of Syk and decreasing the expression of key proteins involved in tumor progression, such as matrix metalloproteinase-2, matrix metalloproteinase-9, and vascular endothelial growth factor. Piceatannol was also shown to inhibit J A K-1, which is a key member of the S T A T pathway that is crucial for controlling cellular activities in response to extracellular cytokines and is a C O X-2-inducible enzyme involved in inflammation and carcinogenesis. Additional research is needed to fully understand the underlying mechanisms of piceatannol's antiproliferative responses against melanoma.
